title: Extractive vs Abstractive Compression
concept: Pulling out key sentences verbatim versus rewriting content more concisely, and the fidelity tradeoffs of each.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, we used an LLM call to shrink a long document into a dense summary before it entered context. What was that process called?
exercises.explain: Right — using an LLM to condense a long document or conversation history into a shorter, denser summary before feeding it into context is called summarization as compression. It reduces token count while trying to preserve the important meaning.

cards.title: Cut It or Rewrite It?
cards.body: Imagine you have to shrink a giant document for an AI's context window. Do you cut out the most important sentences and keep them word-for-word, or do you read it all and rewrite a shorter version in your own words? Both are compression — but they behave very differently.

cards.title: Two Ways to Compress
cards.body: Extractive compression selects and keeps the most important sentences or chunks exactly as written, then drops the rest. Abstractive compression reads the content and generates brand-new, shorter text that captures the meaning in different words.

cards.title: Highlighter vs Rewriter
cards.body: Extractive compression is like using a highlighter on a textbook: you only keep the exact sentences that matter. Abstractive compression is like writing your own study notes in your own words after reading the chapter.

cards.title: A News Article, Two Ways
cards.body: Extractive: pull out the 3 sentences with the most key facts, unchanged, e.g. 'The mayor announced the new budget on Tuesday.' Abstractive: write a fresh sentence like 'City leaders unveiled updated spending plans this week' — same idea, new words, but not a direct quote.

cards.title: Myth: Abstractive Is Always Better
cards.body: It's not — abstractive summaries can accidentally add wrong details or drop small but important facts because the model is generating new phrasing, not copying it. Extractive compression can't 'hallucinate' new words since it only reuses your original text, though it may miss context needed to connect ideas.

cards.title: Extractive vs Abstractive at a Glance
cards.body: Both shrink your text, but they trade off fidelity against fluency in opposite directions.

left.text: Copies exact original sentences; high fidelity, but can feel choppy or disconnected

right.text: Generates new, flowing text; more concise, but risks paraphrasing or small errors

exercises.prompt: Which statement best describes the fidelity tradeoff between extractive and abstractive compression?

options.text: Extractive risks changing facts; abstractive always preserves exact wording
Extractive preserves exact original wording but may lose flow; abstractive can be more concise and coherent but risks paraphrasing errors
Both approaches produce identical output every time
Abstractive compression never uses an LLM, only extractive does

exercises.explain: Extractive compression's strength is faithfulness — it literally reuses source sentences, so wording is never distorted, though the result can feel choppy since sentences are pulled from different spots. Abstractive compression's strength is smooth, compact rewriting, but because it generates new phrasing, there's a real risk of subtly changing meaning or introducing small errors.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Extractive compression can introduce made-up facts that weren't in the original text.
exercises.explain: False — extractive compression only copies existing sentences from the source, so it cannot invent new facts. Abstractive compression is the one more prone to introducing inaccuracies, since it generates new wording that might drift from the original meaning.

exercises.sentence: Extractive compression keeps the original sentences verbatim, while abstractive compression generates new text to express the same meaning.
exercises.explain: Verbatim means 'word for word' — extractive methods copy exact original text rather than rewriting it, which is the key difference from abstractive compression.

exercises.prompt: One of these three compressed summaries was produced by an ABSTRACTIVE (rewriting) method. Which one?

options.text: "The company reported a 12% increase in revenue this quarter," said the CEO in the earnings call.
Revenue climbed sharply this quarter, reflecting strong overall company performance.
Q3 revenue rose 12%, according to CEO remarks during the earnings call.

exercises.explain: Option B uses different wording and phrasing than any direct quote — words like 'climbed sharply' and 'strong overall performance' are paraphrased interpretations, a signature of abstractive rewriting. The other two options preserve exact phrases and numbers straight from a source, which points to extractive selection.

exercises.prompt: Your AI app needs to compress 50 pages of legal contract text into context for a lawyer who must quote exact clauses in court. Which compression approach fits best, and why?

options.text: Abstractive, because shorter is always better regardless of accuracy
Extractive, because exact wording and verifiable quotes matter more than smoothness here
Neither — always send the full 50 pages raw to avoid any tradeoffs
Abstractive, because paraphrased clauses are legally identical to the original text

exercises.explain: When exact wording is legally or factually critical, extractive compression is safer because it preserves the original phrasing verbatim, so nothing gets subtly changed or misattributed. Abstractive summaries are great for compactness and readability, but they rephrase content, which risks altering meaning or losing precise wording — a real problem when someone needs to quote a source exactly. The general lesson: pick extractive when fidelity to source wording matters, and abstractive when you need maximum compression and can tolerate some paraphrasing risk.
